summary: Ronacher reflects on 2010 as a surprisingly productive year, arguing that recaps reveal accomplishments you otherwise underappreciate. He catalogs month-by-month milestones across open source, talks, travel, and side projects, emphasizing learning and experimentation beyond Python web work. The narrative highlights how a joke framework evolved into Flask and how community events and mentoring shaped his year. He also notes the real costs of hardware and travel alongside the benefits of meeting collaborators. The conclusion is modest and forward-looking: a rough 3D voxel engine marks progress and a lot of learning, even if it is not yet a game.
key_points: Recaps help recognize the cumulative value of a year’s work that feels scattered in the moment.
A playful April Fools framework directly led to the creation and release of Flask.
Community events (DjangoCon, EuroPython, PyCon PL) and talks were central to his growth despite nerves.
Open source mentorship and projects (Sphinx/GSOC, pocoo.org) provided tangible impact and pride.
Exploration outside Python web development, especially 3D engine work, drove significant learning.
money_quotes: I find that a wonderful idea because it turns out that after a year you end up doing a lot and don't appreciate it enough.
And it turns out, 2010 was an amazing year.
Released Flask, a microframework based on my April's fool joke.
It didn't go as well as it could have been but I was nervous as fuck, especially because I was the first talk after the keynote.
Relaunched pocoo.org with a new design.
Looked for a bunch of companies using pocoo Software and was proud seeing where our stuff ended up beeing.
I finally have something that looks rougly like a 3D voxel engine.
tone: reflective, informal, candid, and lightly celebratory
key_insight: Looking back at a year’s scattered efforts reveals meaningful growth, community impact, and unexpected outcomes like a joke project becoming a real framework.
